one_liner: A 26-year-old man is brought to the emergency department by his fiancee after nine days of barely sleeping, rapid pressured speech, grandiose plans to 'buy a nightclub chain,' and an impulsive cross-country shopping spree.

stage2.hpi_extension: Nine days ago he abruptly stopped needing sleep, getting by on 1-2 hours per night yet feeling 'more energetic than ever.' His fiancee reports **rapid pressured speech, flight of ideas, and grandiose beliefs** that he will become a billionaire by buying several nightclubs. He emptied their joint savings on impulsive purchases, maxed a credit card on cryptocurrency, started three 'businesses,' and had unprotected sex with a stranger. He has been irritable and easily distracted and feels he has 'special insight' into the stock market. Two years ago he had a 3-month episode of severe depression with anhedonia, guilt, and suicidal ideation that resolved without treatment.
stage2.pmh: Prior untreated depressive episode 2 years ago
stage2.family_history: **Father with bipolar disorder on lithium**; paternal grandfather committed suicide
stage2.social_history: Graphic designer. Engaged. Drinks 1-2 beers/week at baseline but has been drinking more heavily during this episode. Denies illicit drug use.
stage2.physical_exam: General: well-dressed in flamboyant attire, pacing, restless, **hyperverbal with loud pressured speech**. Affect: expansive and irritable. Thought process: **flight of ideas with frequent tangentiality**. Thought content: **grandiose delusions** about becoming a billionaire; denies auditory/visual hallucinations; no suicidal or homicidal ideation. Insight: absent — he does not believe anything is wrong. Neuro exam: non-focal, no tremor or rigidity.

lab_results.result: **Urine toxicology screen negative** for amphetamines, cocaine, cannabinoids, opioids, PCP, and benzodiazepines. Standard urinalysis unremarkable.
lab_results.clinical_significance: Excludes substance-induced mood disorder (especially stimulants, which are the most common mimic of a manic episode) before diagnosing primary bipolar disorder.

lab_results.result: **TSH within normal limits**; free T4 normal. Baseline obtained to monitor for lithium-induced thyroid dysfunction.
lab_results.clinical_significance: Rules out hyperthyroidism, which can mimic mania, and provides a baseline for planned lithium therapy, which commonly causes hypothyroidism.

lab_results.result: Sodium, potassium, chloride, bicarbonate, glucose, BUN, **creatinine normal**, calcium, and liver enzymes all within normal limits. Baseline renal function and electrolytes obtained for lithium or valproate planning.
lab_results.clinical_significance: Baseline renal and hepatic function are required before starting lithium (cleared renally) or valproate (hepatotoxic). Electrolyte disturbances can also mimic behavioral changes.

stage5.final_clue: He is admitted for safety and started on **lithium** for mood stabilization with short-term adjunctive olanzapine for acute agitation. Baseline renal, thyroid, calcium, and cardiac monitoring are documented. By the end of the admission, his sleep normalizes, speech slows, and grandiosity recedes. He is counseled that **antidepressant monotherapy is contraindicated** given the risk of precipitating mania, and that mood stabilization must continue long-term.

debrief.classic_presentation: **Bipolar I disorder** requires **at least one manic episode** — a distinct period of abnormally and persistently elevated, expansive, or irritable mood with increased energy, **lasting ≥1 week (or any duration if hospitalization is required)**, plus **≥3 symptoms of DIG FAST**: **D**istractibility, **I**rresponsibility/impulsivity (indiscretion), **G**randiosity, **F**light of ideas (or racing thoughts), **A**ctivity increase / psychomotor agitation, **S**leep decreased need, **T**alkativeness/pressured speech. Symptoms cause marked impairment, psychotic features, or require hospitalization. **Bipolar II** requires at least one **hypomanic episode** (≥4 days, less severe, no psychosis, no hospitalization) plus at least one major depressive episode. **Cyclothymic disorder** = ≥2 years of subsyndromal hypomanic and depressive symptoms. Must exclude substance-induced and medical causes.
debrief.pathophysiology: Bipolar disorder is a highly **heritable** (~60-80% in twin studies) polygenic illness with dysregulation of monoamines (dopamine, norepinephrine, serotonin), glutamate, and intracellular signaling. Manic states feature increased dopaminergic tone in mesolimbic circuits; depressive states show decreased monoaminergic activity. Disruption of circadian rhythms and sleep architecture is central — sleep deprivation can precipitate mania. Onset is typically in late adolescence to early adulthood. Lithium and other mood stabilizers modulate second-messenger systems (inositol monophosphatase, GSK-3beta) and have neuroprotective effects.
debrief.treatment_overview: **Mood stabilizers** are the cornerstone: **lithium** (gold standard; anti-suicide effect; narrow therapeutic window; **monitor levels, renal function, thyroid, and calcium**; watch for **tremor, polyuria/nephrogenic DI, hypothyroidism, and teratogenicity — Ebstein anomaly**); **valproate** (effective for mixed/rapid-cycling states; **hepatotoxic, teratogenic with neural tube defects, weight gain, pancreatitis**); **carbamazepine** (Stevens-Johnson, aplastic anemia, CYP inducer); **lamotrigine** (good for bipolar depression maintenance; **slow titration to avoid Stevens-Johnson syndrome**). **Atypical antipsychotics** (quetiapine, olanzapine, risperidone, aripiprazole) are used for acute mania and maintenance. **ECT** is effective for severe or refractory mania or depression. **Avoid antidepressant monotherapy** — it can trigger a manic switch. Psychoeducation, sleep hygiene, and adherence are essential.
high_yield_pearls: Mania = **DIG FAST** (Distractibility, Indiscretion, Grandiosity, Flight of ideas, Activity increase, Sleep decreased, Talkativeness) for **≥1 week** — one episode = Bipolar I.
**Lithium** is the gold standard mood stabilizer and uniquely reduces suicide risk; **narrow therapeutic index** — monitor levels, renal function, thyroid, and calcium.
Lithium in pregnancy is associated with **Ebstein anomaly** (atrialized right ventricle); valproate causes **neural tube defects**; both are teratogenic.
**Lamotrigine** is first-line for bipolar depression maintenance but must be **titrated slowly to avoid Stevens-Johnson syndrome**.
**Never give antidepressant monotherapy** in bipolar disorder — it can precipitate a manic episode. Pair with a mood stabilizer.
sources: First Aid for the USMLE Step 1 (2017) — Psychiatry chapter, 'Mood disorders — Bipolar disorder' section.
First Aid for the USMLE Step 1 (2017) — Psychiatry chapter, 'Psychiatric pharmacology — Mood stabilizers (lithium, valproate, lamotrigine, carbamazepine)' section.
